FRIDAY, JUNE 19, 2026 Good news only Updated 6:42 AM Get the daily smile →

Good news only, since 2026 Hand-picked from across the web Verified before it cheers you up

Kindness Animals Everyday Heroes Science Wins Good AI The Planet Community Comebacks Reunions The Big Picture
The Big Picture

Things are better than the headlines suggest.

It rarely makes the front page, because progress is slow and bad news is loud. But step back far enough and the trend is unmistakable: across the long arc, humanity is getting healthier, safer, more literate and less poor. Here's the proof — in the numbers.

Extreme poverty

▼ 90% since 1820
8.5% from 84% in 1820

Share of the world living in extreme poverty. Two centuries ago it was almost everyone; today it's fewer than one in ten.

Source: Our World in Data

Child mortality

▼ 91% since 1800
3.7% from 43% in 1800

Share of children who die before their fifth birthday. In 1800 it was nearly half of all children; it has fallen further in every decade since.

Source: Our World in Data

Life expectancy

▲ 2.5× since 1800
73 yrs from 29 yrs in 1800

How long a newborn can expect to live, worldwide. It has more than doubled since 1800 — the average person now lives into their seventies.

Source: Our World in Data

Literacy

▲ 7.3× since 1820
87% from 12% in 1820

Share of adults who can read and write. Two centuries ago only 1 in 10 could; today nearly 9 in 10 can — more than five billion people.

Source: Our World in Data

Child deaths per year

▼ 62% since 1990
4.9M from 12.8M in 1990

The number of children under five who die each year — most from preventable causes. It has fallen by well over half since 1990.

Source: UN IGME / UNICEF

Homicide in Western Europe

▼ 98% since 1300
1 from 41 in 1300 · per 100k

Murders per 100,000 people. Over seven centuries, lethal violence in everyday life has collapsed to a tiny fraction of what it once was.

Source: Our World in Data (Eisner)

How Cloudbreak works

We comb publishers across the web for the stories that make your day a little brighter, show you the headline and a quick summary, then send you straight to the original source for the full story. We don't republish anyone's work — good news belongs to everyone, and credit belongs to the people who reported it.

Spotted something good?

A neighbor, a rescue, a small win on your street — if it made you smile, send it our way.

Send us a tip →
